population: Adult patients with suspected sepsis in the emergency department of a tertiary care hospital in Brazil
effect_size: qSOFA=1 point p=0.02; qSOFA=2 points p=0.03; qSOFA=3 points p=0.04
performance: Independent factor for ICU admission at all score levels
notes: qSOFA ≥2 present in 58.80% of patients; average age 73±19 years; study period January 2019 to December 2020

population: Adult patients with suspected sepsis in the emergency department of a tertiary care hospital in Brazil
notes: Overall mortality rate was 19.7% (131 patients)

population: Sepsis patients admitted to the emergency department
notes: No association found between severity criteria and mortality

population: Patients presenting to the emergency department with suspected sepsis
sample_size: N=411 (patients scoring at least one severity criterion)
outcome: Tendency towards higher mortality; severe condition at admission
notes: Most patients presented qSOFA ≥ 2 at admission; 53.61% scored only one point on severity criteria at triage

population: Patients presenting to the emergency department with suspected sepsis
timing: From protocol recognition of potential sepsis to ATB start
effect_size: Mean time to ATB start: 37.43 min; ~34% received ATB within 30 min; 58.52% between 30-60 min; ~7% after 60 min
notes: Patients divided into three groups by time of ATB administration

population: Patients presenting to the emergency department with suspected sepsis
effect_size: ~17% 30-day mortality; ~50% ICU admission rate among those who died; ~10-day hospital length of stay
notes: Average age 73.36 years; 75% elderly (≥65 years); predominant infection site respiratory (49.77%), followed by urinary (23.01%), abdominal (12.33%), skin (3.01%)

population: Patients with suspicion of sepsis
notes: Univariate analysis also identified age ≥65 years, qSOFA 3 points, shorter time before ATB administration, and higher lactate values as risk factors for 30-day mortality.

population: Patients with suspicion of sepsis
notes: 30-day deaths: 113 (17%); overall deaths: 131 (19.7%). Positive blood cultures: 98 (14.74%); negative: 505 (75.94%); contaminants: 60 (9.02%).

population: Patients with suspicion of sepsis admitted in the ED
notes: Age ≥65 years, three points on qSOFA at triage, shorter times before ATB administration (p=0.02), higher lactate values (p=0.04), and positive blood cultures (p=0.002) were significant only in univariate analysis.

population: Patients with suspicion of sepsis presenting to the emergency department (ED) triage

notes: Authors concluded qSOFA and severity criteria should not be recommended as tools to predict mortality in patients with suspicion of sepsis in ED triage. qSOFA was considered a valuable predictor of mortality for ICU admission.

population: Patients with suspicion of sepsis presenting to the emergency department (ED) triage

population: Sepsis patients presenting to the Emergency Department (ED)
effect_size: Univariate RR 1.52 (95% CI 1.01–2.31); Multivariate RR 1.27 (95% CI 0.82–1.95)
notes: Age ≥65 was significant on univariate but not multivariate analysis

population: Sepsis patients presenting to the Emergency Department (ED)

notes: qSOFA 3 points was significant on univariate but not multivariate analysis; qSOFA 1 and 2 points were not significant in either analysis

population: Sepsis patients presenting to the Emergency Department (ED)
